channel.title: Recipe Format and Design: Why Cooklang Looks the Way It Does on Cooklang
channel.description: Recent content in Recipe Format and Design: Why Cooklang Looks the Way It Does on Cooklang

item.title: What Goes Into Designing a Recipe Markup Language
item.author: hello@cooklang.org (Alexey)
item.description: Recipes are one of the most structured forms of human writing, yet almost every digital format either loses that structure or buries it in machine syntax. Here's how we designed a markup language that keeps recipes readable and machine-parseable at the same time.

item.title: What Is a Recipe Markup Language? (And Why You'd Want One)
item.author: hello@cooklang.org (Alexey)
item.description: A markup language adds structure to text without making it unreadable. HTML does this for web pages. Cooklang does it for recipes. Here's what that means and why it matters for anyone who cooks.

item.title: Recipe Programming Language — Write and Manage Recipes as Code
item.author: hello@cooklang.org (Alexey)
item.description: Cooklang is a programming language for recipes. Declare ingredients as typed variables, generate shopping lists automatically, scale servings with a command, and version-control your cookbook with Git. Here's how recipes and code are the same thing — and why that matters.

item.title: Why Plain Text Recipes Beat Databases Every Time
item.author: hello@cooklang.org (Alexey)
item.description: After years of building recipe apps with databases, I discovered that plain text files solve the real problems better. Here's why the future of digital recipes isn't in the cloud - it's in your text editor.

item.title: Cooking Timers That Live Inside Your Recipes
item.author: hello@cooklang.org (Alexey)
item.description: Most cooking timers are separate from the recipe. Cooklang embeds timers directly in your instructions — tap a step and the timer starts. Run multiple timers at once, get lock-screen notifications, and set timers up to 999 hours for fermentation and slow processes.

item.title: David A. Mundie on Cooking and Technology
item.author: hello@cooklang.org (Alexey)
item.description: An interview with David A. Mundie, creator of RxOL - the first programming language for recipes in 1985. He shares his vision of simplifying cooking through technology, his thoughts on recipe formats, and perspectives on the future of kitchen automation.

item.title: What Recipe Software Should Tell You About Nutrition
item.author: hello@cooklang.org (Alexey)
item.description: Cooking method changes nutrition as much as the ingredients do. Recipe apps know neither. Here's what structured recipe data could finally make possible.

item.title: Recipe Algorithms: How Recipes Work Like Computer Programs
item.author: hello@cooklang.org (Alexey)
item.description: Every recipe is an algorithm — a sequence of operations transforming inputs into output. Here's the formal model behind cooking instructions, why it matters for recipe validation and automation, and how Cooklang uses it.

item.title: Why Recipes Don't Have a Standard Format (And Why That's a Problem)
item.author: hello@cooklang.org (Alexey)
item.description: Music has notation. Code has formal grammar. Recipes — something humans share daily — still don't have a standard format. Here's why that matters and what a real recipe standard looks like.

item.title: Generating a Recipe Graph with ChatGPT
item.author: hello@cooklang.org (Alexey)
item.description: Using GPT-4 to trace ingredients through cooking steps and generate a recipe graph — a visual representation of how raw ingredients transform into a finished dish.
